text: I am sharing a case of a robotic sigmoid resection for a small cancer in a polyp that was incompletely resected. There was residual disease at the site on a repeat endoscopic view. The patient was offered attempted endoscopic resection versus formal surgical oncologic resection, and they chose oncologic resection. I just burned the area where there was a little tattoo mark. It was kind of underwhelming. You'll see later in the case I do flexible endoscopy to take a look and make sure that's the right spot.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, tracing along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to achieve a high ligation, aided by the patient's minimal visceral fat.

The surgeon visually identifies the left ureter during lateral dissection, which becomes visible without stents. The dissection continues along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) towards its root at the aorta.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter before proceeding with the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The ureter is visible, ensuring it is not inadvertently transected. The surgeon carefully cleans the IMA by dissecting within the vascular sheath and adventitia, revealing a clear plane around the vessel.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, joining the previously completed medial dissection. A sponge is visible, used as a precaution to avoid ureter injury. The redundant sigmoid colon and mesentery are noted, and the splenic flexure is not mobilized.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectosigmoid, ensuring the ureter remains lateral to the surgical field to prevent injury. Using a vessel sealer, the rectus sigmoid junction is cleaned, with the rectum mobilized slightly to facilitate anastomosis. The dissection proceeds underneath the transected inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) up to the bowel wall, with visible lymph nodes indicating a successful yield.

The surgeon uses a flexible endoscope to visualize the tumor, located near the area where the vessel sealer is positioned, ensuring a six-centimeter proximal margin. Perfusion is checked, and the decision is made against transrectal extraction of the specimen to avoid tearing the rectum.

The surgeon divides the bowel proximally and distally, ensuring a ninety-degree angle across the anti-mesenteric surface. A stay suture is placed in the far corner to align the staple line, followed by the use of a 3-0 nine-inch V-lock suture to join the two staple lines, forming the posterior outer row of a two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis, using scissors to make a proctotomy followed by an enterotomy. The outer back row of the anastomosis is completed, and the surgeon prepares to use ICG to assess perfusion, ensuring there is no ischemia near the stapeline.

The surgeon begins sewing the inner posterior row of the anastomosis, starting at the posterior midline and stitching away from themselves. Each stitch is cinched after placement to ensure precise alignment.

The surgeon takes a bite on the rectal side and parks the suture in the pelvis, then starts a new stitch in the middle of the posterior row, sewing towards themselves. The lip created at the enterotomy, about a centimeter away from the staple line, is sutured using a baseball stitch technique, guiding each suture precisely around the corner.

The surgeon transitions back to the outer layer of the anastomosis, using the inner stitch to sew away from themselves, inverting the outside lip effectively. The suture technique involves navigating around a corner, leading to a successful inversion of the anterior row. The surgeon ties the two layers together, completing the anastomosis with a focus on maintaining the integrity of the anterior row.
The surgeon ties the suture to the silk stay suture to complete the anastomosis. A flexible endoscope is inserted through the anastomosis to perform a leak test, checking for patency and bleeding.

No trouble coming up and through that anastomosis, widely patent. This patient did very well, was discharged on post-operative day one. Again, pathology T one and zero was doing very well at her two week follow up. Specimen was subsequently at the end of the case extracted through a fin and steel incision and was discharged with five pills of oxycodone, which they said they didn't take. Thank you for watching.
